revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:隼神社 (奈良市)]]
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Hayabusa Shrine (Tsunofuri Myōjin) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Hayabusa Jinja Nara.jpg | location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, Tsunofurishinyachō 44 | deity = {{ill|Hayabusa Sobetsu no Mikoto|ja|隼総別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Sobetsu no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = | consecration_year = | Main Hall = | festival = August 1 | Cultural Properties = Wooden Seated Statue of Jizō Bosatsu (Nara City Designated Cultural Property) | Latitude = 34 | Latitude minutes = 40 | Latitude seconds = 54.39 | Longitude = 135 | Longitude minutes = 49 | Longitude seconds = 39.50 | Map = Nara city | location = {{ill|Nara|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.681775|N|135.8276389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Hayabusa Shrine''' (隼神社), also known as '''Tsunofuri Myōjin''' or '''Tsunofuri Hayabusa Myōjin''', is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. It is also referred to as '''Tsubakimoto Shrine''' (椿本神社) in some historical records.<ref name="shishi">Nara City History - Shrines and Temples Edition, p.180</ref><ref name="kadokawa">Kadokawa Japanese Place name Dictionary: Nara Prefecture, p.727</ref> The shrine gives its name to the Tsunofuri area of Nara City.<ref name="kadokawa" />
== History == According to historical records, the shrine was originally located on {{ill|Kasugayama|en|Kasugayama|qid=Q11513600|lt=Kasuga Mountain|1=Kasugayama}} and was known as the Tsubakimoto Shrine.<ref name="shishi" /> The original structure was destroyed by fire during the Jishō 4 ({{ill|1180|en|1180|qid=Q19702|lt=1180|1=1180}}) conflicts, and only the sacred tree remained, which was subsequently venerated.<ref name="shishi" />
A small shrine dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} was later built to the east of the sacred tree and is now worshipped as Munakata Shrine.<ref name="shishi" /> Historical documents note that the shrine burned again during the great fire of Kōji 4 (1278) at {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}, after which it became a smaller shrine.<ref name="shishi" />
It is said that the shrine was partially relocated to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} during the relocation of the capital to Heian-kyō.<ref name="shishi" />
== Enshrined Deities == * {{ill|Hayabusa Sobetsu no Mikoto|ja|隼総別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Sobetsu no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - The deity is considered the child of {{ill|Hosuseri no Mikoto|ja|火酢芹命|1=Hosuseri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosuseri no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Hosuseri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 火須勢理命}} and is worshipped alongside his father.<ref name="shishi" />
== Grounds == * Sacred Tree - A dead persimmon tree with a {{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}} is revered as a sacred tree. A new persimmon tree has also been planted nearby.<ref name="shishi" /> In the past, a willow tree was also considered sacred.<ref name="shishi" /> * Stone Lantern - A stone lantern dating to Kyōhō 4 (1719).<ref name="shishi" /> * Auxiliary Shrine - Munakata Shrine, dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima-hime|ja|市杵島姫命|1=Ichikishima-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima-hime||1=Ichikishima-hime|2=fr|3=Ichikishimahime|4=ja|5=イチキシマヒメ|6=zh|7=市寸島比賣命|8=ru|9=Итикишима-химэ|10=de|11=Ichikishima-hime}}.<ref name="shishi" />
=== Enmei Jizō === [[File:Enmei Jizouson Nara.jpg|thumb|Enmei Jizō]] A statue known as the Enmei Jizō is enshrined in the shrine's administrative office.<ref name="enmei">Nara City History - Shrines and Temples Edition, p.209</ref> The statue is usually housed in a locked {{ill|zushi|ja|厨子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zushi|qq=}} with inner doors painted with depictions of the {{ill|Four Heavenly Kings|en|Four Heavenly Kings|qid=Q1068367|lt=Four Heavenly Kings|1=Four Heavenly Kings}}.<ref name="enmei" /> A monthly ceremony is conducted on the 24th by the head priest of {{ill|Amida-ji Temple|ja|阿弥陀寺 (奈良市南風呂町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amida-ji Temple|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://otera.jodo.or.jp/temple/30-010/ Amida-ji Temple]</ref>
The wooden seated statue, measuring 73 cm, depicts a half-seated {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu|1=Kṣitigarbha}} in the popular late Heian style.<ref name="annai">Nara City Board of Education, onsite plaque</ref> Made of {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|qid=Q917187|lt=Chamaecyparis obtusa|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} wood using the {{ill|yosegi zukuri|ja|寄木造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yosegi zukuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏像|1=Buddha in art|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1000809}} technique, the eyes feature {{ill|gyokugan|ja|玉眼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gyokugan|qq=}} inlays, and the clothing displays fine Kamakura-style patterns.<ref name="annai" /> The halo and base are later additions.<ref name="annai" />
It was designated a Nara City Cultural Property on March 3, 1988.<ref name="annai" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Nara City History Editorial Committee, ''Nara City History - Shrines and Temples Edition'', Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985 * Kadokawa Place name Encyclopedia Editorial Committee, ''Kadokawa Japanese Place name Dictionary: Nara Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, March 1990, ISBN 9784040012902 * Onsite Information Plaque by Nara City Board of Education
{{shinto-stub}} {{buddhism-stub}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{pref-stub-Nara}}
